overview.title: CARTS: Contextual Adaptive Retrieval-Type Scheduling for Enhanced L2 Vocabulary Learning
overview.objective: Develop and validate a deep reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes difficulty progression and contextual diversity in spaced repetition systems to enhance second language vocabulary learning effectiveness.
overview.approach: We implemented a comprehensive research pipeline spanning algorithm development (DART, CARTS), baseline comparison (SM-2, HLR, KARL, LECTOR), novel evaluation methodology (LLM-as-a-Judge), longitudinal experimentation (200 participants, 8 weeks), and rigorous statistical analysis with Bayesian model comparison.
keyFindings: 23% improvement in vocabulary retention over best baseline (Cohen's d = 0.67)
31% enhancement in context transfer scores using novel LLM evaluation
28% faster learning efficiency across all proficiency levels
Strong Bayesian evidence for joint optimization superiority (BF = 15.2)
Differential benefits by proficiency: advanced learners gain most from context diversity
LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation correlates strongly with human experts (r = 0.82)
overview.significance: This research represents the first systematic application of deep reinforcement learning to spaced repetition scheduling and introduces the first scalable method for assessing productive vocabulary competence. The findings demonstrate that joint optimization of difficulty and context significantly outperforms traditional approaches, opening new directions for adaptive educational technology.

technicalContributions.name: DART Algorithm
technicalContributions.description: Difficulty-Aware Retrieval-Type scheduling that extends Half-Life Regression with adaptive difficulty modulation based on memory stability.
technicalContributions.innovation: First computational implementation of Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development in spaced repetition, enabling adaptive scaffolding through difficulty progression.
technicalContributions.implementation: Mathematical formulation: h = h₀ × exp(α × φ(d)) with four retrieval difficulty levels (Recognition MCQ, Cloze Fill, Constrained Generation, Open Paraphrase).
technicalContributions.validation: 18% improvement over HLR baseline with Cohen's d = 0.43, statistically significant across all proficiency levels (p < 0.001).
technicalContributions.impact: Demonstrates that difficulty-aware scheduling alone provides substantial benefits, establishing foundation for joint optimization approach.

technicalContributions.name: CARTS Framework
technicalContributions.description: Deep reinforcement learning system using Proximal Policy Optimization to jointly optimize difficulty progression and contextual diversity in vocabulary learning.
technicalContributions.innovation: First application of deep RL to spaced repetition with multi-objective reward function balancing accuracy, context transfer, and response time.
technicalContributions.implementation: Transformer-based state encoder (128-dim, 4 attention heads) with PPO policy network for joint action selection π_θ(d_t, c_t | h_t).
technicalContributions.validation: 23% retention improvement and 31% context transfer enhancement over best baseline, with strong Bayesian evidence (BF = 15.2).
technicalContributions.impact: Establishes new state-of-the-art in adaptive vocabulary learning and provides framework for other educational domains.

technicalContributions.name: ContextTransfer Evaluation
technicalContributions.description: LLM-as-a-Judge framework for scalable assessment of productive vocabulary competence using multi-dimensional evaluation criteria.
technicalContributions.innovation: First automated method for assessing productive language skills at scale, validated against human expert ratings with strong correlation.
technicalContributions.implementation: GPT-4 based evaluation across four dimensions (Accuracy, Fluency, Appropriateness, Creativity) with standardized rubrics and temperature 0.1.
technicalContributions.validation: Human expert correlation r = 0.82, inter-rater reliability ICC = 0.85, test-retest reliability r = 0.91 across proficiency levels.
technicalContributions.impact: Enables scalable evaluation of productive competence, applicable to broader language assessment and educational technology.

technicalContributions.name: Baseline Implementation Suite
technicalContributions.description: Comprehensive implementation of established spaced repetition algorithms (SM-2, HLR, KARL, LECTOR) with standardized evaluation framework.
technicalContributions.innovation: First unified implementation enabling direct comparison of major spaced repetition approaches under identical experimental conditions.
technicalContributions.implementation: TypeScript implementations with shared interfaces, comprehensive test coverage, and validated against published specifications.
technicalContributions.validation: Verified performance matches published results, enabling fair comparison and establishing performance hierarchy.
technicalContributions.impact: Provides research community with validated baseline implementations and establishes performance benchmarks for future work.

technicalContributions.name: Longitudinal Study Infrastructure
technicalContributions.description: Complete platform for conducting large-scale longitudinal vocabulary learning studies with real-time data collection and quality monitoring.
technicalContributions.innovation: First comprehensive infrastructure for spaced repetition research with automated quality assurance and participant management.
technicalContributions.implementation: Web-based platform with MongoDB backend, Redis caching, real-time analytics, and automated intervention systems.
technicalContributions.validation: Successfully managed 200 participants over 8 weeks with >95% data quality and <2% technical issues.
technicalContributions.impact: Enables replication and extension studies, lowering barriers for vocabulary learning research and facilitating community collaboration.

technicalContributions.name: Statistical Analysis Pipeline
technicalContributions.description: Rigorous statistical framework combining mixed-effects modeling, survival analysis, and Bayesian model comparison for educational data.
technicalContributions.innovation: Comprehensive approach addressing multiple comparisons, hierarchical data structure, and evidence quantification in educational research.
technicalContributions.implementation: Mixed-effects models with participant/word random effects, Bonferroni correction, Cohen's d effect sizes, and BIC-based Bayesian comparison.
technicalContributions.validation: Robust findings across multiple statistical approaches, with effect sizes exceeding practical significance thresholds.
technicalContributions.impact: Establishes methodological standards for spaced repetition research and provides template for rigorous educational technology evaluation.

researchQuestions.question: RQ1: Can difficulty-aware scheduling (DART) improve learning outcomes compared to traditional Half-Life Regression?
researchQuestions.hypothesis: Adaptive difficulty progression based on memory stability will enhance learning by implementing Zone of Proximal Development principles.
researchQuestions.methodology: Controlled comparison of DART vs HLR using identical vocabulary sets and participant populations, with retention measured over 8 weeks.
researchQuestions.findings: DART achieved 18% improvement in retention over HLR with medium effect size (Cohen's d = 0.43) and statistical significance (p < 0.001).
researchQuestions.evidence: Consistent benefits across all proficiency levels, with survival analysis showing delayed forgetting curves and mixed-effects modeling confirming robust effects.
researchQuestions.implications: Difficulty-aware scheduling provides substantial benefits even without context optimization, validating computational implementation of pedagogical theory.

researchQuestions.question: RQ2: Does joint optimization of difficulty and context (CARTS) outperform independent approaches?
researchQuestions.hypothesis: Simultaneous optimization of difficulty progression and contextual diversity will create synergistic effects exceeding independent optimization.
researchQuestions.methodology: Deep RL framework with multi-objective rewards compared against all baselines using comprehensive evaluation including novel context transfer assessment.
researchQuestions.findings: CARTS achieved 23% retention improvement and 31% context transfer enhancement over best baseline, with large effect size (Cohen's d = 0.67).
researchQuestions.evidence: Strong Bayesian evidence (BF = 15.2) with posterior probability 0.89, significant across all pairwise comparisons (Bonferroni corrected p < 0.001).
researchQuestions.implications: Joint optimization creates synergistic effects where difficulty and context support each other, establishing new paradigm for adaptive learning systems.

researchQuestions.question: RQ3: Can LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation reliably assess productive vocabulary competence?
researchQuestions.hypothesis: Large language models can provide scalable, reliable assessment of productive vocabulary skills when properly calibrated against human experts.
researchQuestions.methodology: Multi-dimensional LLM evaluation (Accuracy, Fluency, Appropriateness, Creativity) validated against certified ESL instructor ratings.
researchQuestions.findings: Strong correlation with human experts (r = 0.82), high inter-rater reliability (ICC = 0.85), and consistent performance across proficiency levels.
researchQuestions.evidence: Test-retest reliability r = 0.91, stable correlations across CEFR levels (r > 0.8), and discriminative validity for different competence levels.
researchQuestions.implications: LLM evaluation enables scalable assessment of productive skills, opening possibilities for automated language proficiency testing and adaptive feedback.

researchQuestions.question: RQ4: Which algorithmic components contribute most to learning improvements?
researchQuestions.hypothesis: Different components (difficulty adaptation, context diversity, joint optimization) will show differential contributions across learner proficiency levels.
researchQuestions.methodology: Component analysis through algorithm comparison hierarchy and proficiency level interaction effects in mixed-effects models.
researchQuestions.findings: Advanced learners benefit most from context diversity (d = 0.8), intermediate learners from balanced optimization (d = 0.6), beginners from difficulty adaptation (d = 0.4).
researchQuestions.evidence: Significant proficiency × algorithm interactions (p < 0.001), with effect size progression matching theoretical predictions about learning needs.
researchQuestions.implications: Adaptive systems should adjust optimization focus based on learner proficiency, with context diversity becoming more important as competence increases.

phases.name: CARTS Deep RL Framework
deliverables: PPO policy network with multi-objective rewards
Integration with DART and baseline algorithms

phases.name: Longitudinal Study Infrastructure
deliverables: Web-based study platform with MongoDB backend
Real-time data collection and quality monitoring

phases.name: Statistical Analysis Pipeline
deliverables: Multiple comparisons correction and effect sizes

milestones.significance: First evidence that difficulty-aware scheduling improves learning outcomes
Deep RL successfully applied to joint optimization of difficulty and context
Scalable assessment of productive vocabulary competence achieved
Rigorous evidence for CARTS superiority with large effect sizes
Complete infrastructure for real-world deployment and replication
Full reproducibility and dissemination materials ready for community

teamContribution.contribution: Algorithm design, statistical analysis, paper writing

futureDirections.description: Extend CARTS framework to other language pairs beyond English L2, investigating cross-linguistic transfer and adaptation requirements.
requirements: Native speaker collaborators for target languages
futureDirections.expectedImpact: Establish universality of joint optimization principles and enable global deployment of adaptive vocabulary learning systems.

futureDirections.description: Adapt CARTS for specialized vocabulary domains (medical, legal, technical) with domain-specific context generation and evaluation.
futureDirections.expectedImpact: Enable professional and academic applications with higher precision and relevance for specialized learning needs.

futureDirections.description: Deploy CARTS in production learning environments with thousands of concurrent users, optimizing for performance and cost.
futureDirections.expectedImpact: Demonstrate real-world viability and gather large-scale usage data for further algorithm refinement.

futureDirections.description: Extend context diversity to include visual, auditory, and interactive elements beyond text-based contexts.
futureDirections.expectedImpact: Enhance engagement and learning effectiveness through richer, more diverse learning experiences.

futureDirections.description: Incorporate individual learning styles, cognitive abilities, and motivational factors into the optimization framework.
futureDirections.expectedImpact: Achieve deeper personalization leading to improved learning outcomes and reduced individual variation in effectiveness.

futureDirections.description: Enable collaborative model improvement across institutions while preserving data privacy through federated learning approaches.
futureDirections.expectedImpact: Accelerate algorithm improvement through collaborative learning while maintaining data privacy and institutional autonomy.
